title: ASML Eyes Higher Lithography Tool Prices as TSMC Pushes Back, Highlighting Growing Cost Tensions in Chip Manufacturing
text:
AVAQ Semiconductor
New member

ASML is moving to raise prices for parts of its semiconductor equipment portfolio, reflecting strong demand for advanced lithography systems and the company's growing pricing power. However, the proposed increases are reportedly facing resistance from Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (TSMC), underscoring the mounting cost pressures across the semiconductor manufacturing supply chain.

During its second-quarter 2026 earnings call, ASML management said the company sees further room for price improvements as demand for its lithography platforms continues to outpace supply. According to CFO Roger Dassen, the market environment has strengthened ASML's pricing position, with discussions on higher pricing already underway with customers. The company's EUV production capacity is now nearly fully booked through the end of 2027, giving ASML greater confidence in its long-term pricing strategy.

Despite the favorable market conditions, negotiations with key customers appear to be far from straightforward. Reports indicate that TSMC has resisted ASML's proposed price increases, particularly as the foundry continues to carefully manage capital expenditures for next-generation manufacturing equipment. TSMC has also remained cautious about adopting ASML's High-NA EUV systems, which carry a price tag of approximately US$400 million per tool. Industry reports suggest the company does not expect to introduce the technology even for its A13 manufacturing process, currently planned around 2029.

While advanced EUV systems remain the industry's most expensive manufacturing tools, pricing pressure is not limited to leading-edge equipment. Reports indicate that several Chinese customers have already accepted price increases of around 10% for ASML's mature deep ultraviolet (DUV) lithography systems. The willingness of these customers to absorb higher costs reflects continued demand for DUV equipment used in mature-node production, particularly as domestic semiconductor manufacturing capacity continues to expand.

ASML's pricing initiative comes as the company continues to strengthen its financial outlook. Following its latest quarterly results, the Dutch lithography leader raised its 2026 revenue guidance for the second time this year. The company also announced plans to expand EUV production capacity by roughly 30% annually over the next two years while increasing output of DUV systems to support customer demand across multiple technology nodes.

The pricing discussions illustrate a broader shift taking place within the semiconductor equipment industry. As AI-driven demand accelerates investment in advanced manufacturing capacity, equipment suppliers are gaining greater leverage in commercial negotiations. At the same time, leading semiconductor manufacturers continue balancing technology leadership with increasingly expensive capital investment decisions.

Whether ASML succeeds in broadly implementing higher equipment prices may ultimately depend on customer-specific investment strategies. While demand for cutting-edge lithography remains exceptionally strong, major foundries such as TSMC are expected to continue scrutinizing equipment costs as they plan future process technology roadmaps.

Source: Trendforce

Author: Avaq Semiconductor Engineer

Avaq is a global electronic components and semiconductor distributor founded in Hong Kong in 2013. It helps businesses source electronic parts such as integrated circuits (ICs), sensors, connectors, and other components. The company works with a large network of suppliers and serves customers in more than 80 countries.
